nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes and drop-offs; forms large aggregations along upper drop-off edges at depths of 2–40 m

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on zooplankton, planktonic crustaceans, and fish eggs in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer a variety of frozen and live foods including mysid shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and other zooplankton substitutes. Frequent small feedings (3–5 times daily) have been recommended to maintain body condition.

avoidWith: other male anthias of the same species

summary: A striking deep-pinkish-to-purple reef anthias from Indo-Pacific outer reef slopes, generally considered expert-level due to demanding feeding requirements and sensitivity to stress. Best kept as a small group with one male and several females.
careNotes:
Queen Purple Anthias tend to be among the more challenging anthias species to maintain long-term in captivity. Sources consistently rate them as expert-only, noting a pronounced tendency to refuse prepared foods and susceptibility to bacterial infections and parasites when stressed or underfed. Frequent small feedings (3–5 times daily) are generally advised, with a variety of live and frozen zooplankton-type foods. A well-established, stable system with excellent water quality has been reported to improve long-term survival odds.

This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite: all individuals are born female and a dominant individual transitions to male. Haremic social groups (one male with several females) are the natural social structure and are recommended in captivity. A single specimen is generally considered less viable long-term than a small group. Males display a pointed snout, a dark blotch on the rear dorsal fin, extended caudal fin lobes, and taller second dorsal and anal fin rays compared to females.

Taxonomy note: LiveAquaria and some older sources list this species under Serranidae, while FishBase and current ichthyology place it in the family Anthiadidae (a split from Serranidae accepted by most current authorities).

Temperature disagreement: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22–25.6°C); FishBase records a wild range of 22–28°C. The value used here (22–27°C) averages the two.
breedingNotes: Has been documented as a protogynous hermaphrodite with harem social structure. Captive breeding has been reported as extremely difficult; spawning in well-maintained large systems may occur but rearing larvae to settlement is not reliably documented in home aquaria.

additional.name: fishesofaustralia.net.au
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm TL confirmed), native range, taxonomy, and hermaphrodite/haremic behavior.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm TL), temperature range (22–28°C vs LiveAquaria 22–25.6°C — averaged to 22–27°C), and confirmed family Anthiadidae as current taxonomy. Reef2Reef wiki returned HTTP 403.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been documented as a protogynous hermaphrodite with harem social structure. Spawning in large established systems may occur but reliable larval rearing is not documented for home aquaria.
